focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

There were fewer things that could stop a Meridian West train than people said, and after watching one version of its dirt cross into other hands, Marisol kept circling the others. She knew most of them by smell before she knew them by code. Hot brake lining gave off a scorched-penny stink when a shoe dragged too long. A blackwater tank near overflow sweetened the air in a way bleach never quite reached, toilet chemical floating on top of old shit and heat. Preservative from the trauma room lived at the back of the tongue like bad fruit and a coin held too long in the mouth. New hires thought a line could be stopped by a man collapsing in restraints, by screaming, by a child with a fever, by the sight of blood on the floor. Meridian West rolled through all of that. What stopped it, reliably, was liability that belonged to somebody else.

The clinic exam bay at Caldera Junction had been built to suggest care and had settled, over the years, into storage. A cracked blood-pressure cuff hung from a hook. The examination light had lost one of its handles and stayed aimed at the corner as if punishing the mop bucket. Paloma's pathology cooler sat on the tile between their feet, white plastic gone yellow at the edges, the latch wrapped in a strip of green dish towel because it chewed the palm when she carried it. Every time the compressor kicked, the lid rattled softly. Marisol heard the glass inside tap each other and thought of all the ways a body could be turned into paperwork.

She had spread three documents across the stainless prep table: the dispatch-safe checklist, a waste-management escalation sheet, and a restraint inventory form with the old company logo ghosting through the new one. On the back of an expired meal count she had written three headings i...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...dock, cold on her jaw. Marisol took a breath that smelled of creosote and clogged drains and fried oil and all the other things the company spent money trying to rename.

"A hearing," she said. "That's all tonight. I need the bigger map. The real one. What failures stay ours. What failures force the world in. What breaks first under strain and what breaks where cameras can see it. I need you to hear the larger plan before I decide whether I can live with asking anybody to help me do it."

Ivo rubbed the back of his neck. For a second he looked older than forty-six, every shift and crawlspace and overtime summer sitting plain on him. Then the deadpan came back around the edges, not enough to hide him, just enough to let him keep standing.

"Jesus Christ, Sol."

"Yes."

"You really picked a romantic night."

"The coffee was bad enough to count as ambiance."

That got the smallest snort out of him. He looked at the train again, then at the dorm windows, then down at his own taped hand as if checking what still belonged to him.

"Tomorrow after first maintenance whistle," he said. "Machine shop annex. Teresa won't come in there unless the place is actively on fire."

Marisol kept still.

"That isn't me saying I'm in," he added.

"I know."

"It's me saying I'll hear it. The whole ugly thing. You get one pass at telling it straight."

The words should have felt like relief. They did not. They landed with the weight of a deduction already taken. Across the yard the dorm windows burned on in the rain, company rooms paid one withheld check at a time, and Marisol understood with a new exactness that every hand she asked for would come to her already pledged to rent, medicine, visitation days, pension years, or some other ordinary piece of life RailPassage had priced to the cent.
